title: China Reopens Boeing Order Book — LEAP Engine Supply Chain Squeeze
catalyst: On May 14, 2026, President Trump announced that China agreed to purchase 200 Boeing 737 jets during the Trump-Xi summit in Beijing — China's first major US commercial aircraft order in nearly a decade. The order layers on top of Boeing's plan to lift 737 MAX production to 47/month in 2026 with a stretch target of 53/month by year-end, while CFM International (GE Aerospace + Safran) is already targeting a record 2,000+ LEAP engine deliveries against a multi-year backlog. Engine production — not airframe assembly — is now the binding constraint on global narrowbody output.
thesis: The market reacted to the announcement by selling Boeing down 4% on a smaller-than-expected order, missing the second-order effect: every incremental 737 MAX requires two LEAP-1B engines plus engineered structural components, and the supplier base is sold out years ahead of the airframer. China's order doesn't help Boeing's near-term margins — it tightens an already strained CFM and Howmet order book at higher utilization, which is where the operating leverage actually sits. Engine OEMs and structural casting/forging suppliers capture the volume ramp regardless of which airline takes delivery.

ticker_rationale.GE: GE Aerospace owns 50% of CFM International, the exclusive engine supplier on the 737 MAX. 2025 LEAP output hit a record 1,800+ units (+25% YoY) on a $190B backlog, and the company is committing $1B in 2026 to expand manufacturing — $200M specifically on high-pressure turbine components for LEAP. A China-driven order surge converts directly into more LEAP shipsets and decades of high-margin aftermarket revenue at ~3-4x install-base economics. GE scores 84 (BUY) in our IOWN screen.
ticker_rationale.HWM: Howmet Aerospace supplies engineered titanium structures, fastening systems, and engine airfoils to both Boeing airframes and CFM LEAP engines — content per 737 MAX is roughly 2x prior generation, and the company already flagged commercial-aero build-rate recovery as a primary tailwind. FY2025 adj EBITDA grew 26% on 11% revenue growth with 2026 guidance for another ~10% revenue growth before this catalyst. China resuming Boeing deliveries pulls forward demand at peak operating leverage. HWM scores 74 (HOLD) in our IOWN screen.

risks: Trade deal could unwind quickly — the May 2026 truce expires November 10, 2026, and a re-escalation could see China cancel or defer the Boeing order before deliveries begin

Boeing remains the production bottleneck despite the order — FAA quality oversight, fuselage defect rework, or another labor disruption could cap 737 MAX deliveries below the supplier-funded ramp rate
LEAP engine durability issues (hot-section wear in dusty operating environments) could trigger costly warranty reserves that offset CFM volume gains
Order is for 200 jets vs Wall Street's expectation of up to 500 — if follow-on orders fail to materialize, supplier upside may already be priced in to GE
sources: Bloomberg, 'Boeing Wins 200-Jet Plane Order From China, Trump Says', May 14, 2026
